'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 水谷神社 |画像 = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
鳥居 |所在地 = {{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|養父市|Q869014}}奥米地字中島235 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|天照皇大神}} |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（名神大）・{{jalink|但馬国|Q1053727}}{{jalink|三宮|Q7418864}}・旧{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}} |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = 一間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}板葺 |別名 = |札所 = |例祭 = 10月体育の日 |神事 = ほいやら踊（笹踊）・ネッテイ相撲（共に例祭日） |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=水谷神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''水谷神社'''（みずたにじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|養父市|Q869014}}の{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。 「みたに」神社とも通称される。奥米地集落の北西部、3段に分かたれた境内地の最上段に東面して鎮座する。{{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964|式内名神大社}}で、{{jalink|旧社格}}は{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}。 === 祭神 === {{jalink|天照皇大神}}を祀るとされるが、「水谷」は「水垂」で{{jalink|水神|Q3137449}}とする説{{jalink|佐伯有義|Q11382847}}編『{{jalink|神祇全書}}』（明治39-41年）。、「水谷」は「みたに」と訓み「御谷」の謂であるとする説{{jalink|鈴鹿連胤|Q6536446}}『{{jalink|神社覈録|Q11590708}}』（明治3年）。、{{jalink|保食神|Q2280323}}とする説『養父郡誌』（昭和3年）所引「縁起」。この縁起は{{jalink|弘化|Q1325317|弘化年中}}（19世紀半ば）に記されたものという。、等もある。 === 由緒 === 創祀年代等不明。保食神が人々に{{jalink|稲}}を始めとする五穀の栽培法を教えたため、地名を「米地」と称し、同神を祀ったものとの説がある前掲「縁起」。なお「米地（めいじ）」は「目路」の意味で、目通しの狭い地形に基づく地名であるともいう（『角川日本地名大辞典 28兵庫県』、角川書店、昭和63年）。。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に名神大社として記載される古社で但し『延喜式』臨時祭（巻3）名神祭条には不載。、当初は現鎮座地の南方、奥米地と中米地の境界を成す谷を「水谷」といい、その谷奥に懸かる滝の更に上部に鎮座していたという『兵庫県の地名Ⅰ』。。 {{jalink|但馬国|Q1053727}}の水谷神社は、{{jalink|弘安|Q389180}}8年（{{jalink|1285年|Q5546}}）の『{{jalink|但馬国大田文}}』に「水谷社」、{{jalink|嘉元|Q1327409}}4年（{{jalink|1306年|Q5716}}）の『{{jalink|昭慶門院}}領目録案』に「水谷大社」等と見えるが、これは今の{{jalink|養父神社|Q11666856}}の事と説かれ{{jalink|桜井舟山}}『{{jalink|但馬考}}』（江戸時代中期）。、何時の頃からか養父神社に{{jalink|合祀|Q11412524}}されて「養父水谷大明神」と号されたために混同されたものと解され、また奥米地にはなお{{jalink|和魂}}を祀る神社が残されていたので、後にそこへ再度分祀されたものとも推定されている『養父郡誌』。。その後大嵐によって社殿が流失、その社殿が流れ着いた{{jalink|米地川}}沿いの平地において奉斎される事となったが、{{jalink|宝永|Q1145262}}7年（{{jalink|1710年|Q6861}}）に現在地へ遷座されたという『兵庫県の地名Ⅰ』。上掲「縁起」は遷座の年を{{jalink|享保|Q1145048}}2年（1717）としているが、現本殿に宝永7年の墨書銘が残されている。。 {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）10月に村社に列せられた。 === 祭祀 === 例祭はもと9月9日（{{jalink|旧暦|Q11511066}}）に行われ、その後10月17日となり、同月の23日乃至は24日へと変遷を重ね、現在は10月{{jalink|体育の日}}に行われる。境内社田和神社の秋祭りも兼ね久下隆史「兵庫の祭礼行事・解説 - 奥米地水谷神社のネッテイ祭り」（『祭礼行事・兵庫県』所収）。、例祭後に'''ほいやら踊'''と'''ネッテイ相撲'''、子供相撲の奉納が行われる。 === ほいやら踊 === 本殿における例祭に引き続いて奉納される。'''笹踊り'''とも呼ばれ、田和神社前で{{jalink|袷|Q11626642}}に{{jalink|兵児帯|Q11392799}}を締め、腰に手拭いと{{jalink|煙草入}}を提げて{{jalink|下駄|Q721941}}を履き、長さ3{{jalink|メートル|Q11573}}程の{{jalink|篠竹}}を手にした12人の男性によって踊られる。 初めに踊り手が輪になって並び、下駄を脱いでその上に立つ。次いで篠竹の元で地面を打ちつつ「ヨイ、ヨイ、ヨイ、ヨイ」と掛け声を掛けながら下駄の上を移動し、左廻りに3周して自分の下駄が置かれた位置に戻るが、その間1人でも下駄を踏み外せば初めからやり直す。次いで下駄を履き、輪の中央を向いて竹を左手に執り右手で腰の手拭いの有無を確認し、竹を右手に持ち替えて左手で煙草入を確認（「一服する」所作であるという）、異状無ければ両手で竹の元と末を執り撓めて輪にし、「ヤッ、ヤッ、ヤッ」の掛け声に合わせて3度地面を打つ。最後に田和神社の神前に竹を奉納して踊りを終え、ネッテイ相撲へ移る『養父のまつり』。。なお、この踊りは旧{{jalink|養父町 (兵庫県養父郡2004年)|Q11666855|養父町}}下では唯一の神事踊りであった。 === ネッテイ相撲 === まず{{jalink|裃|Q290445}}姿（但し上半身は下衣無し）に素足で{{jalink|刀|Q7523706}}を手にした成人男子2人が、{{jalink|袴|Q164668}}の{{jalink|股立}}を執り刀を水谷神社本殿前へ捧げて拝礼し、拝礼後{{jalink|神主|Q15888735}}から刀を受け取って石段を境内中段へ降る。中段では{{jalink|肩衣}}を脱ぎ、それで刀を巻いて両手で捧げ持ち、降りて来た石段へ向かって3歩両足飛びをし、刀を石段へ置き右手で刀を押さえ左手で腰を撫でる。次いで左右手を入れ替えて同じ所作をし、最後に再度右手で刀を押さえ左手で腰を撫で、3歩下がって中段中央でお互いに向き合う。中段広場には{{jalink|土俵|Q832429}}が作られる事もないが、軽く一礼をした後に{{jalink|相撲|Q40561}}と同じ仕切りの姿勢を取り、以下のように進行する。 #「ヨイ、ヨイ、ヨイ」の掛け声で左右左と{{jalink|四股|Q109364591}}を踏み、「ヨイ」と声を掛けて右足を左前に踏み出しながら右手を拳に握って突き出す #同様に四股を踏み、「ヨイ」と声を掛けて左右逆の所作をする #同様に四股を踏み、左手で相手の首を抱き右手は腰に当てて、「ヨイ、ヨイ、ヨイ」の掛け声で跳躍しながら左に廻り元の位置に戻る #同様に四股を踏み、左右の手を入れ替えて同様に右に1廻り #最後に同様の四股を踏み、両手で相手の肩を抱き同様に左に1廻りして元の位置へ戻り、両手を腰に当てて左右に一礼して終える 相撲を終えた両人は石段の上に置いた刀を肩衣を巻いたまま両手に捧げ持ち、本殿前へ昇ってそれを拝殿へ納め、引き続き境内最下段に設らえた土俵で子供相撲が行われる。 かつては相撲の取り手は次に見る「宮当番」から「御当（おとう）」とも呼ばれた{{jalink|頭屋|Q11664931|頭人（とうにん）}}が選ばれて勤めており、相撲の後には拝殿で翌年の頭人を決める籤引きと{{jalink|神酒|Q3313722}}を酌み交わす「お当渡し」が行われたが、現在では宮当番とは関係無くほぼ決まった人物が行っている文化庁・総務省、「[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 養父のネッテイ相撲]」（文化遺産オンライン）（平成22年4月20日閲覧）。。 ネッテイ相撲は勝負を決するものでなく、土俵も{{jalink|行司|Q907647}}も無い特殊な{{jalink|相撲|Q40561}}となっており、これは土地の悪霊を鎮めるとともに作物の稔りを感謝する神事、或いは{{jalink|呪術|Q14637843}}的な相撲神事と見られ三隅治雄編『全国年中行事辞典』、東京堂出版、平成19年。 、{{jalink|平安時代|Q193292}}に{{jalink|宮中|Q11453221}}で行なわれた{{jalink|相撲節会|Q11582050}}の流れを汲む現行の相撲の原型ともされ水谷神社氏子中「ネッテイ相撲」（平成20年10月吉日付の境内説明板）。、「養父のネッテイ相撲」という名称で{{jalink|平成|Q234510}}15年（{{jalink|2003年|Q1986}}）2月20日に国の「{{jalink|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|Q11642584}}」に選択され、翌16年3月9日には兵庫県の{{jalink|無形民俗文化財}}に指定にされている。 なお、現行下では勝負を決する形ではないが、かつては最後に肩を組んで左右に廻った後に元の位置へ正確に戻った方を勝ちとしたようで、そこからこれは作物の豊凶を占ったり、部落として何らかの決定をなす等の二者択一の選択を行なう必要が生じた場合に、代表者を選んで勝負をさせ、その結果を以て神意を伺った{{jalink|うけい|Q11260316|誓約（うけい）}}に起源を持つものではないかとも説かれる。それによると、この相撲で拳を突き出すのは{{jalink|じゃんけん|Q106631}}で、「あいこ」で勝負が着かない時に肩を組んで廻る勝負となったもので、土俵が無いのはそのため、また行司がいないのは観衆によって容易に判定が下され得たためではないかという。また、「ネッテイ」は「にって相撲」「ねったえ相撲」の転訛とも言われ、当地方で繰り返す事を「練って」と呼ぶために四股を何度も繰り返す事に由来するとも、「練る」ような相撲に由来するとも言われる。 === 宮座 === 現在神社の管理は{{jalink|氏子|Q125562175}}各戸を10組に分け、各組が1年交替で神社の掃除や祭典の{{jalink|神饌|Q11591045}}準備、ネッテイ相撲の世話等を行ない（以前は1組宛が勤めていたが現今では2組宛）、これを「宮当番」と称している。かつては「水谷講」という講が組まれて講員の中から宮当番が選ばれ、宮当番は神社の有する宮田（みやでん）7{{jalink|畝|Q2262727}}を耕作して祭りの費用の一部に充てていた。 === 社殿 === {{jalink|本殿|Q2044994}}は{{jalink|流造|Q210684|一間社流造}}板葺で、正側面の3方に{{jalink|擬宝珠|Q3105399}}{{jalink|高欄}}付きの縁（大床）を廻らし、正面に設けた昇段階下に{{jalink|浜縁}}（浜床）を張る。規模は小さいものの{{jalink|覆屋}}内にあるために保存状態は良好。墨書銘から宝永7年の建造物である事が判り、細部の彫刻に独特の作風が見られる『兵庫県大百科事典』下巻、神戸新聞出版センター、昭和58年。。その覆屋は{{jalink|切妻造|Q13308575}}平入{{jalink|瓦葺}}で、同じく切妻造瓦葺で妻入の{{jalink|拝殿|Q3125745}}（明治42年（1909年）6月17日建造）が接する。境内最下段に建つ公民館はかつて{{jalink|日待ち}}のための通夜等も行われた舞堂を改築したもの『式内社調査報告』。。 === 境内社 === 本殿左手（南）に三柱神社と田和神社が、右手に山野口神社と八柱神社が鎮座する。田和神社は{{jalink|大正|Q329174}}2年（{{jalink|1913年|Q2080}}）に奥米地字田和から境内へ遷祠されたもので、三柱神社は字古堂から、三柱神社は字井ノ口から遷祠された。 === 文化財 === *ネッテイ相撲（国の選択無形民俗文化財、兵庫県指定無形民俗文化財） name in kana (P1814) == * みずたにじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7140005010305 == Commons category (P373) == * Mizutani-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県養父市奥米地字中島235', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 水谷神社 |画像 = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
鳥居 |所在地 = {{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|養父市|Q869014}}奥米地字中島235 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|天照皇大神}} |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（名神大）・{{jalink|但馬国|Q1053727}}{{jalink|三宮|Q7418864}}・旧{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}} |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = 一間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}板葺 |別名 = |札所 = |例祭 = 10月体育の日 |神事 = ほいやら踊（笹踊）・ネッテイ相撲（共に例祭日） |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=水谷神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''水谷神社'''（みずたにじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}{{jalink|養父市|Q869014}}の{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。 「みたに」神社とも通称される。奥米地集落の北西部、3段に分かたれた境内地の最上段に東面して鎮座する。{{jalink|名神大社|Q9610964|式内名神大社}}で、{{jalink|旧社格}}は{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}。 === 祭神 === {{jalink|天照皇大神}}を祀るとされるが、「水谷」は「水垂」で{{jalink|水神|Q3137449}}とする説{{jalink|佐伯有義|Q11382847}}編『{{jalink|神祇全書}}』（明治39-41年）。、「水谷」は「みたに」と訓み「御谷」の謂であるとする説{{jalink|鈴鹿連胤|Q6536446}}『{{jalink|神社覈録|Q11590708}}』（明治3年）。、{{jalink|保食神|Q2280323}}とする説『養父郡誌』（昭和3年）所引「縁起」。この縁起は{{jalink|弘化|Q1325317|弘化年中}}（19世紀半ば）に記されたものという。、等もある。 === 由緒 === 創祀年代等不明。保食神が人々に{{jalink|稲}}を始めとする五穀の栽培法を教えたため、地名を「米地」と称し、同神を祀ったものとの説がある前掲「縁起」。なお「米地（めいじ）」は「目路」の意味で、目通しの狭い地形に基づく地名であるともいう（『角川日本地名大辞典 28兵庫県』、角川書店、昭和63年）。。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に名神大社として記載される古社で但し『延喜式』臨時祭（巻3）名神祭条には不載。、当初は現鎮座地の南方、奥米地と中米地の境界を成す谷を「水谷」といい、その谷奥に懸かる滝の更に上部に鎮座していたという『兵庫県の地名Ⅰ』。。 {{jalink|但馬国|Q1053727}}の水谷神社は、{{jalink|弘安|Q389180}}8年（{{jalink|1285年|Q5546}}）の『{{jalink|但馬国大田文}}』に「水谷社」、{{jalink|嘉元|Q1327409}}4年（{{jalink|1306年|Q5716}}）の『{{jalink|昭慶門院}}領目録案』に「水谷大社」等と見えるが、これは今の{{jalink|養父神社|Q11666856}}の事と説かれ{{jalink|桜井舟山}}『{{jalink|但馬考}}』（江戸時代中期）。、何時の頃からか養父神社に{{jalink|合祀|Q11412524}}されて「養父水谷大明神」と号されたために混同されたものと解され、また奥米地にはなお{{jalink|和魂}}を祀る神社が残されていたので、後にそこへ再度分祀されたものとも推定されている『養父郡誌』。。その後大嵐によって社殿が流失、その社殿が流れ着いた{{jalink|米地川}}沿いの平地において奉斎される事となったが、{{jalink|宝永|Q1145262}}7年（{{jalink|1710年|Q6861}}）に現在地へ遷座されたという『兵庫県の地名Ⅰ』。上掲「縁起」は遷座の年を{{jalink|享保|Q1145048}}2年（1717）としているが、現本殿に宝永7年の墨書銘が残されている。。 {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）10月に村社に列せられた。 === 祭祀 === 例祭はもと9月9日（{{jalink|旧暦|Q11511066}}）に行われ、その後10月17日となり、同月の23日乃至は24日へと変遷を重ね、現在は10月{{jalink|体育の日}}に行われる。境内社田和神社の秋祭りも兼ね久下隆史「兵庫の祭礼行事・解説 - 奥米地水谷神社のネッテイ祭り」（『祭礼行事・兵庫県』所収）。、例祭後に'''ほいやら踊'''と'''ネッテイ相撲'''、子供相撲の奉納が行われる。 === ほいやら踊 === 本殿における例祭に引き続いて奉納される。'''笹踊り'''とも呼ばれ、田和神社前で{{jalink|袷|Q11626642}}に{{jalink|兵児帯|Q11392799}}を締め、腰に手拭いと{{jalink|煙草入}}を提げて{{jalink|下駄|Q721941}}を履き、長さ3{{jalink|メートル|Q11573}}程の{{jalink|篠竹}}を手にした12人の男性によって踊られる。 初めに踊り手が輪になって並び、下駄を脱いでその上に立つ。次いで篠竹の元で地面を打ちつつ「ヨイ、ヨイ、ヨイ、ヨイ」と掛け声を掛けながら下駄の上を移動し、左廻りに3周して自分の下駄が置かれた位置に戻るが、その間1人でも下駄を踏み外せば初めからやり直す。次いで下駄を履き、輪の中央を向いて竹を左手に執り右手で腰の手拭いの有無を確認し、竹を右手に持ち替えて左手で煙草入を確認（「一服する」所作であるという）、異状無ければ両手で竹の元と末を執り撓めて輪にし、「ヤッ、ヤッ、ヤッ」の掛け声に合わせて3度地面を打つ。最後に田和神社の神前に竹を奉納して踊りを終え、ネッテイ相撲へ移る『養父のまつり』。。なお、この踊りは旧{{jalink|養父町 (兵庫県養父郡2004年)|Q11666855|養父町}}下では唯一の神事踊りであった。 === ネッテイ相撲 === まず{{jalink|裃|Q290445}}姿（但し上半身は下衣無し）に素足で{{jalink|刀|Q7523706}}を手にした成人男子2人が、{{jalink|袴|Q164668}}の{{jalink|股立}}を執り刀を水谷神社本殿前へ捧げて拝礼し、拝礼後{{jalink|神主|Q15888735}}から刀を受け取って石段を境内中段へ降る。中段では{{jalink|肩衣}}を脱ぎ、それで刀を巻いて両手で捧げ持ち、降りて来た石段へ向かって3歩両足飛びをし、刀を石段へ置き右手で刀を押さえ左手で腰を撫でる。次いで左右手を入れ替えて同じ所作をし、最後に再度右手で刀を押さえ左手で腰を撫で、3歩下がって中段中央でお互いに向き合う。中段広場には{{jalink|土俵|Q832429}}が作られる事もないが、軽く一礼をした後に{{jalink|相撲|Q40561}}と同じ仕切りの姿勢を取り、以下のように進行する。 #「ヨイ、ヨイ、ヨイ」の掛け声で左右左と{{jalink|四股|Q109364591}}を踏み、「ヨイ」と声を掛けて右足を左前に踏み出しながら右手を拳に握って突き出す #同様に四股を踏み、「ヨイ」と声を掛けて左右逆の所作をする #同様に四股を踏み、左手で相手の首を抱き右手は腰に当てて、「ヨイ、ヨイ、ヨイ」の掛け声で跳躍しながら左に廻り元の位置に戻る #同様に四股を踏み、左右の手を入れ替えて同様に右に1廻り #最後に同様の四股を踏み、両手で相手の肩を抱き同様に左に1廻りして元の位置へ戻り、両手を腰に当てて左右に一礼して終える 相撲を終えた両人は石段の上に置いた刀を肩衣を巻いたまま両手に捧げ持ち、本殿前へ昇ってそれを拝殿へ納め、引き続き境内最下段に設らえた土俵で子供相撲が行われる。 かつては相撲の取り手は次に見る「宮当番」から「御当（おとう）」とも呼ばれた{{jalink|頭屋|Q11664931|頭人（とうにん）}}が選ばれて勤めており、相撲の後には拝殿で翌年の頭人を決める籤引きと{{jalink|神酒|Q3313722}}を酌み交わす「お当渡し」が行われたが、現在では宮当番とは関係無くほぼ決まった人物が行っている文化庁・総務省、「[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 養父のネッテイ相撲]」（文化遺産オンライン）（平成22年4月20日閲覧）。。 ネッテイ相撲は勝負を決するものでなく、土俵も{{jalink|行司|Q907647}}も無い特殊な{{jalink|相撲|Q40561}}となっており、これは土地の悪霊を鎮めるとともに作物の稔りを感謝する神事、或いは{{jalink|呪術|Q14637843}}的な相撲神事と見られ三隅治雄編『全国年中行事辞典』、東京堂出版、平成19年。 、{{jalink|平安時代|Q193292}}に{{jalink|宮中|Q11453221}}で行なわれた{{jalink|相撲節会|Q11582050}}の流れを汲む現行の相撲の原型ともされ水谷神社氏子中「ネッテイ相撲」（平成20年10月吉日付の境内説明板）。、「養父のネッテイ相撲」という名称で{{jalink|平成|Q234510}}15年（{{jalink|2003年|Q1986}}）2月20日に国の「{{jalink|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|Q11642584}}」に選択され、翌16年3月9日には兵庫県の{{jalink|無形民俗文化財}}に指定にされている。 なお、現行下では勝負を決する形ではないが、かつては最後に肩を組んで左右に廻った後に元の位置へ正確に戻った方を勝ちとしたようで、そこからこれは作物の豊凶を占ったり、部落として何らかの決定をなす等の二者択一の選択を行なう必要が生じた場合に、代表者を選んで勝負をさせ、その結果を以て神意を伺った{{jalink|うけい|Q11260316|誓約（うけい）}}に起源を持つものではないかとも説かれる。それによると、この相撲で拳を突き出すのは{{jalink|じゃんけん|Q106631}}で、「あいこ」で勝負が着かない時に肩を組んで廻る勝負となったもので、土俵が無いのはそのため、また行司がいないのは観衆によって容易に判定が下され得たためではないかという。また、「ネッテイ」は「にって相撲」「ねったえ相撲」の転訛とも言われ、当地方で繰り返す事を「練って」と呼ぶために四股を何度も繰り返す事に由来するとも、「練る」ような相撲に由来するとも言われる。 === 宮座 === 現在神社の管理は{{jalink|氏子|Q125562175}}各戸を10組に分け、各組が1年交替で神社の掃除や祭典の{{jalink|神饌|Q11591045}}準備、ネッテイ相撲の世話等を行ない（以前は1組宛が勤めていたが現今では2組宛）、これを「宮当番」と称している。かつては「水谷講」という講が組まれて講員の中から宮当番が選ばれ、宮当番は神社の有する宮田（みやでん）7{{jalink|畝|Q2262727}}を耕作して祭りの費用の一部に充てていた。 === 社殿 === {{jalink|本殿|Q2044994}}は{{jalink|流造|Q210684|一間社流造}}板葺で、正側面の3方に{{jalink|擬宝珠|Q3105399}}{{jalink|高欄}}付きの縁（大床）を廻らし、正面に設けた昇段階下に{{jalink|浜縁}}（浜床）を張る。規模は小さいものの{{jalink|覆屋}}内にあるために保存状態は良好。墨書銘から宝永7年の建造物である事が判り、細部の彫刻に独特の作風が見られる『兵庫県大百科事典』下巻、神戸新聞出版センター、昭和58年。。その覆屋は{{jalink|切妻造|Q13308575}}平入{{jalink|瓦葺}}で、同じく切妻造瓦葺で妻入の{{jalink|拝殿|Q3125745}}（明治42年（1909年）6月17日建造）が接する。境内最下段に建つ公民館はかつて{{jalink|日待ち}}のための通夜等も行われた舞堂を改築したもの『式内社調査報告』。。 === 境内社 === 本殿左手（南）に三柱神社と田和神社が、右手に山野口神社と八柱神社が鎮座する。田和神社は{{jalink|大正|Q329174}}2年（{{jalink|1913年|Q2080}}）に奥米地字田和から境内へ遷祠されたもので、三柱神社は字古堂から、三柱神社は字井ノ口から遷祠された。 === 文化財 === *ネッテイ相撲（国の選択無形民俗文化財、兵庫県指定無形民俗文化財） name in kana (P1814) == * みずたにじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7140005010305 == Commons category (P373) == * Mizutani-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県養父市奥米地字中島235', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Yabu City|Q869014}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Amaterasu Okami}} |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Meishin University), {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}}{{jalink|Sannomiya|Q7418864}}, old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Yabu City|Q869014}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|Shikiuchi Meishin Taisha}}, {{jalink|former company rank}} is {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{jalink|Suijin|Q3137449}}{{jalink|Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847}}, ed. {{jalink|Jingi Zensho}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{jalink|Hoshokugami|Q2280323}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{jalink|Kōka|Q1325317|Kōka Nenchu}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}} was founded in {{jalink|Koan|Q389180}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{jalink|Ota Bun of Tajima Province}}'' in 1994 ({{jalink|Kamoto|Q1327409}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{jalink|Shokeimonin}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{jalink|Fuzan Sakurai}} "{{jalink|Tajima Ko}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{jalink|enshrined|Q11412524}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{jalink|Jakon}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{jalink|Yoneji River}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{jalink|Hoei|Q1145262}}7 ({{jalink|1710|Q6861}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{jalink|lunar calendar|Q11511066}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{jalink|Sports Day}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{jalink|heiko belt|Q11392799}} on their {{jalink|hōji belt|Q11392799}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{jalink|geta|Q721941}} and carrying {{jalink|shinotake}} about 3 {{jalink|meters|Q11573}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{jalink|Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|Yabu Town}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{jalink|katana|Q290445}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{jalink|katana|Q7523706}} in {{jalink|hakama|Q1 64668}}'s {{jalink|Matadate}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{jalink|Shinto priest|Q15888735}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{jalink|shoulder robe}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{jalink|sumo ring|Q832429}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{jalink|sumo|Q40561}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{jalink|four forks|Q109364591}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{jalink|headman|Q11664931|headman}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{jalink|sacred sake|Q3313722}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{jalink|sumo|Q40561}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{jalink|gyoji|Q907647}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{jalink|Magic|Q14637843}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{jalink|sumo festival|Q11582050}} that was held in {{jalink|the imperial court|Q11453221}} during the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{jalink|ukei|Q11260316|pledge (ukei)}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{jalink|rock-paper-scissors|Q106631}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{jalink|parishin|Q125562175}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{jalink|divine food|Q11591045}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{jalink|une|Q2262727}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is {{jalink|Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|Ichikensha Nagaruzo}} board roof, with {{jalink|pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399}}{{jalink|Hamaedoko}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{jalink|hamadoko}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{jalink|covered building}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{jalink|gable-zukuri|Q13308575}} flat with a {{jalink|tiled roof}}, and it is adjacent to the {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{jalink|Himachi}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1913|Q2080}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property) === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *"Shikiuchisha Investigation Report" Volume 19 San'in-do 2, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1982 *"Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Journal" Volume 2 (published by name in kana (P1814) == * みずたにじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7140005010305 == Commons category (P373) == * Mizutani-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県養父市奥米地字中島235', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Yabu City|Q869014}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Amaterasu Okami}} |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Meishin University), {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}}{{jalink|Sannomiya|Q7418864}}, old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Yabu City|Q869014}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|Shikiuchi Meishin Taisha}}, {{jalink|former company rank}} is {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{jalink|Suijin|Q3137449}}{{jalink|Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847}}, ed. {{jalink|Jingi Zensho}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{jalink|Hoshokugami|Q2280323}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{jalink|Kōka|Q1325317|Kōka Nenchu}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}} was founded in {{jalink|Koan|Q389180}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{jalink|Ota Bun of Tajima Province}}'' in 1994 ({{jalink|Kamoto|Q1327409}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{jalink|Shokeimonin}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{jalink|Fuzan Sakurai}} "{{jalink|Tajima Ko}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{jalink|enshrined|Q11412524}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{jalink|Jakon}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{jalink|Yoneji River}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{jalink|Hoei|Q1145262}}7 ({{jalink|1710|Q6861}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{jalink|lunar calendar|Q11511066}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{jalink|Sports Day}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{jalink|heiko belt|Q11392799}} on their {{jalink|hōji belt|Q11392799}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{jalink|geta|Q721941}} and carrying {{jalink|shinotake}} about 3 {{jalink|meters|Q11573}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{jalink|Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|Yabu Town}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{jalink|katana|Q290445}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{jalink|katana|Q7523706}} in {{jalink|hakama|Q1 64668}}'s {{jalink|Matadate}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{jalink|Shinto priest|Q15888735}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{jalink|shoulder robe}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{jalink|sumo ring|Q832429}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{jalink|sumo|Q40561}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{jalink|four forks|Q109364591}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{jalink|headman|Q11664931|headman}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{jalink|sacred sake|Q3313722}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{jalink|sumo|Q40561}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{jalink|gyoji|Q907647}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{jalink|Magic|Q14637843}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{jalink|sumo festival|Q11582050}} that was held in {{jalink|the imperial court|Q11453221}} during the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{jalink|ukei|Q11260316|pledge (ukei)}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{jalink|rock-paper-scissors|Q106631}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{jalink|parishin|Q125562175}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{jalink|divine food|Q11591045}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{jalink|une|Q2262727}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is {{jalink|Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|Ichikensha Nagaruzo}} board roof, with {{jalink|pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399}}{{jalink|Hamaedoko}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{jalink|hamadoko}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{jalink|covered building}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{jalink|gable-zukuri|Q13308575}} flat with a {{jalink|tiled roof}}, and it is adjacent to the {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{jalink|Himachi}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1913|Q2080}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property) === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *"Shikiuchisha Investigation Report" Volume 19 San'in-do 2, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1982 *"Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Journal" Volume 2 (published by the Hyogo Prefecture Shinto Association, reprinted in 1932), Rinsen Shoten, 1981 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 *"Hyogo Prefecture Place Names I" (Japan Historical Place Names Series 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 *Hideo Takahashi and Takashi Kushita (eds.) “Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture”, Ofuu, 1997 ISBN 4-273-02506-X *"Adoptive Father's Festival" (Yabu Town Cultural Properties Series (6)), Yabu Town Board of Education, 1971 === Related items === *{{jalink|List of Shikinai companies in Tajima country|Q11381626}} === External link === {{Commonscat}} Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "Cultural Heritage Online") {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Redirect belongs to category | redirect = Netty Sumo | 1 = Hyogo prefecture name in kana (P1814) == * みずたにじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7140005010305 == Commons category (P373) == * Mizutani-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県養父市奥米地字中島235', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Yabu City|Q869014}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Amaterasu Okami}} |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Meishin University), {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}}{{jalink|Sannomiya|Q7418864}}, old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Yabu City|Q869014}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|Shikiuchi Meishin Taisha}}, {{jalink|former company rank}} is {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{jalink|Suijin|Q3137449}}{{jalink|Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847}}, ed. {{jalink|Jingi Zensho}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{jalink|Hoshokugami|Q2280323}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{jalink|Kōka|Q1325317|Kōka Nenchu}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}} was founded in {{jalink|Koan|Q389180}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{jalink|Ota Bun of Tajima Province}}'' in 1994 ({{jalink|Kamoto|Q1327409}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{jalink|Shokeimonin}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{jalink|Fuzan Sakurai}} "{{jalink|Tajima Ko}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{jalink|enshrined|Q11412524}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{jalink|Jakon}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{jalink|Yoneji River}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{jalink|Hoei|Q1145262}}7 ({{jalink|1710|Q6861}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{jalink|lunar calendar|Q11511066}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{jalink|Sports Day}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{jalink|heiko belt|Q11392799}} on their {{jalink|hōji belt|Q11392799}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{jalink|geta|Q721941}} and carrying {{jalink|shinotake}} about 3 {{jalink|meters|Q11573}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{jalink|Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|Yabu Town}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{jalink|katana|Q290445}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{jalink|katana|Q7523706}} in {{jalink|hakama|Q1 64668}}'s {{jalink|Matadate}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{jalink|Shinto priest|Q15888735}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{jalink|shoulder robe}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{jalink|sumo ring|Q832429}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{jalink|sumo|Q40561}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{jalink|four forks|Q109364591}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{jalink|headman|Q11664931|headman}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{jalink|sacred sake|Q3313722}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{jalink|sumo|Q40561}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{jalink|gyoji|Q907647}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{jalink|Magic|Q14637843}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{jalink|sumo festival|Q11582050}} that was held in {{jalink|the imperial court|Q11453221}} during the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{jalink|ukei|Q11260316|pledge (ukei)}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{jalink|rock-paper-scissors|Q106631}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{jalink|parishin|Q125562175}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{jalink|divine food|Q11591045}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{jalink|une|Q2262727}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is {{jalink|Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|Ichikensha Nagaruzo}} board roof, with {{jalink|pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399}}{{jalink|Hamaedoko}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{jalink|hamadoko}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{jalink|covered building}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{jalink|gable-zukuri|Q13308575}} flat with a {{jalink|tiled roof}}, and it is adjacent to the {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{jalink|Himachi}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1913|Q2080}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property) === Footnotes === 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Yabu City|Q869014}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Amaterasu Okami}} |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Meishin University), {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}}{{jalink|Sannomiya|Q7418864}}, old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Yabu City|Q869014}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{jalink|Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|Shikiuchi Meishin Taisha}}, {{jalink|former company rank}} is {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{jalink|Suijin|Q3137449}}{{jalink|Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847}}, ed. {{jalink|Jingi Zensho}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{jalink|Hoshokugami|Q2280323}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{jalink|Kōka|Q1325317|Kōka Nenchu}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}} was founded in {{jalink|Koan|Q389180}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{jalink|Ota Bun of Tajima Province}}'' in 1994 ({{jalink|Kamoto|Q1327409}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{jalink|Shokeimonin}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{jalink|Fuzan Sakurai}} "{{jalink|Tajima Ko}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{jalink|enshrined|Q11412524}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{jalink|Jakon}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{jalink|Yoneji River}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{jalink|Hoei|Q1145262}}7 ({{jalink|1710|Q6861}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{jalink|lunar calendar|Q11511066}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{jalink|Sports Day}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{jalink|heiko belt|Q11392799}} on their {{jalink|hōji belt|Q11392799}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{jalink|geta|Q721941}} and carrying {{jalink|shinotake}} about 3 {{jalink|meters|Q11573}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{jalink|Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|Yabu Town}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{jalink|katana|Q290445}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{jalink|katana|Q7523706}} in {{jalink|hakama|Q1 64668}}'s {{jalink|Matadate}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{jalink|Shinto priest|Q15888735}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{jalink|shoulder robe}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{jalink|sumo ring|Q832429}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{jalink|sumo|Q40561}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{jalink|four forks|Q109364591}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{jalink|headman|Q11664931|headman}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{jalink|sacred sake|Q3313722}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{jalink|sumo|Q40561}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{jalink|gyoji|Q907647}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{jalink|Magic|Q14637843}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{jalink|sumo festival|Q11582050}} that was held in {{jalink|the imperial court|Q11453221}} during the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{jalink|ukei|Q11260316|pledge (ukei)}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{jalink|rock-paper-scissors|Q106631}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{jalink|parishin|Q125562175}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{jalink|divine food|Q11591045}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{jalink|une|Q2262727}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is {{jalink|Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|Ichikensha Nagaruzo}} board roof, with {{jalink|pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399}}{{jalink|Hamaedoko}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{jalink|hamadoko}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{jalink|covered building}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{jalink|gable-zukuri|Q13308575}} flat with a {{jalink|tiled roof}}, and it is adjacent to the {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{jalink|Himachi}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{jalink|Taisho|Q329174}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1913|Q2080}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property) === Footnotes ===
