== name in kana (P1814) == * みずたにじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7140005010305 == Commons category (P373) == * Mizutani-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県養父市奥米地字中島235', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Okami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Meishin University), {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}{{ill|lt=Sannomiya|Q7418864|WD=Q7418864|1=Sannomiya}}, old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Shikinaisha (Myojin Taisha)|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534}} is {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{ill|lt=Suijin|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}{{ill|lt=Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, ed. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jingi Zensho|1=UNKNOWN}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{ill|lt=Hoshokugami|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|1=Ukemochi}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|lt=Kōka Nenchu|1=Kōka}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Koan|Q389180|WD=Q389180|1=Kōan (Kamakura period)}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ota Bun of Tajima Province|1=UNKNOWN}}'' in 1994 ({{ill|lt=Kamoto|Q1327409|WD=Q1327409|1=Kagen}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shokeimonin|1=UNKNOWN}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fuzan Sakurai|1=UNKNOWN}} "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajima Ko|1=UNKNOWN}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{ill|lt=enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jakon|1=UNKNOWN}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoneji River|1=UNKNOWN}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1710|Q6861|WD=Q6861|1=1710}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{ill|lt=heiko belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=heiko belt}} on their {{ill|lt=hōji belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=hōji belt}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{ill|lt=geta|Q721941|WD=Q721941|1=Geta (footwear)}} and carrying {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinotake|1=UNKNOWN}} about 3 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|1=Metre}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{ill|lt=Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|WD=Q11666855|lt=Yabu Town|1=Yabu}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{ill|lt=katana|Q290445|WD=Q290445|1=kamishimo}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{ill|lt=katana|Q7523706|WD=Q7523706|1=Single-edged sword}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hakama|1=UNKNOWN}}'s {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matadate|1=UNKNOWN}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shoulder robe|1=UNKNOWN}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{ill|lt=sumo ring|Q832429|WD=Q832429|1=Dohyō}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{ill|lt=four forks|Q109364591|WD=Q109364591|1=shiko}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{ill|lt=headman|Q11664931|WD=Q11664931|lt=headman|1=touya}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{ill|lt=sacred sake|Q3313722|WD=Q3313722|1=Omiki}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{ill|lt=gyoji|Q907647|WD=Q907647|1=Gyōji}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{ill|lt=Magic|Q14637843|WD=Q14637843|1=Magic}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{ill|lt=sumo festival|Q11582050|WD=Q11582050|1=Sumai no Sechie}} that was held in {{ill|lt=the imperial court|Q11453221|WD=Q11453221|1=Kyūchū}} during the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{ill|lt=ukei|Q11260316|WD=Q11260316|lt=pledge (ukei)|1=Ukehi}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{ill|lt=rock-paper-scissors|Q106631|WD=Q106631|1=Rock paper scissors}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{ill|lt=parishin|Q125562175|WD=Q125562175|1=Ujiko}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{ill|lt=divine food|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{ill|lt=une|Q2262727|WD=Q2262727|1=Hilling}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{ill|lt=Main shrine|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Ichikensha Nagaruzo|1=Nagare-zukuri}} board roof, with {{ill|lt=pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399|WD=Q3105399|1=Giboshi}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hamaedoko|1=UNKNOWN}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hamadoko|1=UNKNOWN}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=covered building|1=UNKNOWN}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{ill|lt=gable-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} flat with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tiled roof|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is adjacent to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himachi|1=UNKNOWN}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1913|Q2080|WD=Q2080|1=1913}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property) === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *"Shikiuchisha Investigation Report" Volume 19 San'in-do 2, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1982 *"Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Journal" Volume 2 (published by the Hyogo Prefecture Shinto Association, reprinted in 1932), Rinsen Shoten, 1981 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 *"Hyogo Prefecture Place Names I" (Japan Historical Place Names Series 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 *Hideo Takahashi and Takashi Kushita (eds.) “Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture”, Ofuu, 1997 ISBN 4-273-02506-X *"Adoptive Father's Festival" (Yabu Town Cultural Properties Series (6)), Yabu Town Board of Education, 1971 === Related items === *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinai companies in Tajima country|Q11381626|WD=Q11381626|1=List of Shikinaisha in Tajima Province}} === External link === {{Commonscat}} *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6324077.html Mizutani Shrine] (Hyogo Prefectural Shrine Agency) 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Okami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Meishin University), {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}{{ill|lt=Sannomiya|Q7418864|WD=Q7418864|1=Sannomiya}}, old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Shikinaisha (Myojin Taisha)|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534}} is {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{ill|lt=Suijin|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}{{ill|lt=Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, ed. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jingi Zensho|1=UNKNOWN}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{ill|lt=Hoshokugami|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|1=Ukemochi}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|lt=Kōka Nenchu|1=Kōka}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Koan|Q389180|WD=Q389180|1=Kōan (Kamakura period)}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ota Bun of Tajima Province|1=UNKNOWN}}'' in 1994 ({{ill|lt=Kamoto|Q1327409|WD=Q1327409|1=Kagen}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shokeimonin|1=UNKNOWN}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fuzan Sakurai|1=UNKNOWN}} "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajima Ko|1=UNKNOWN}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{ill|lt=enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jakon|1=UNKNOWN}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoneji River|1=UNKNOWN}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1710|Q6861|WD=Q6861|1=1710}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{ill|lt=heiko belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=heiko belt}} on their {{ill|lt=hōji belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=hōji belt}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{ill|lt=geta|Q721941|WD=Q721941|1=Geta (footwear)}} and carrying {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinotake|1=UNKNOWN}} about 3 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|1=Metre}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{ill|lt=Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|WD=Q11666855|lt=Yabu Town|1=Yabu}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{ill|lt=katana|Q290445|WD=Q290445|1=kamishimo}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{ill|lt=katana|Q7523706|WD=Q7523706|1=Single-edged sword}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hakama|1=UNKNOWN}}'s {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matadate|1=UNKNOWN}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shoulder robe|1=UNKNOWN}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{ill|lt=sumo ring|Q832429|WD=Q832429|1=Dohyō}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{ill|lt=four forks|Q109364591|WD=Q109364591|1=shiko}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{ill|lt=headman|Q11664931|WD=Q11664931|lt=headman|1=touya}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{ill|lt=sacred sake|Q3313722|WD=Q3313722|1=Omiki}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{ill|lt=gyoji|Q907647|WD=Q907647|1=Gyōji}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{ill|lt=Magic|Q14637843|WD=Q14637843|1=Magic}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{ill|lt=sumo festival|Q11582050|WD=Q11582050|1=Sumai no Sechie}} that was held in {{ill|lt=the imperial court|Q11453221|WD=Q11453221|1=Kyūchū}} during the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{ill|lt=ukei|Q11260316|WD=Q11260316|lt=pledge (ukei)|1=Ukehi}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{ill|lt=rock-paper-scissors|Q106631|WD=Q106631|1=Rock paper scissors}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{ill|lt=parishin|Q125562175|WD=Q125562175|1=Ujiko}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{ill|lt=divine food|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{ill|lt=une|Q2262727|WD=Q2262727|1=Hilling}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{ill|lt=Main shrine|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Ichikensha Nagaruzo|1=Nagare-zukuri}} board roof, with {{ill|lt=pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399|WD=Q3105399|1=Giboshi}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hamaedoko|1=UNKNOWN}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hamadoko|1=UNKNOWN}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=covered building|1=UNKNOWN}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{ill|lt=gable-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} flat with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tiled roof|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is adjacent to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himachi|1=UNKNOWN}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1913|Q2080|WD=Q2080|1=1913}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property) === Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === *"Shikiuchisha Investigation Report" Volume 19 San'in-do 2, Kogakkan University Publishing Department, 1982 *"Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Journal" Volume 2 (published by the Hyogo Prefecture Shinto Association, reprinted in 1932), Rinsen Shoten, 1981 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 *"Hyogo Prefecture Place Names I" (Japan Historical Place Names Series 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 *Hideo Takahashi and Takashi Kushita (eds.) “Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture”, Ofuu, 1997 ISBN 4-273-02506-X *"Adoptive Father's Festival" (Yabu Town Cultural Properties Series (6)), Yabu Town Board of Education, 1971 === Related items === *{{ill|lt=List of Shikinai companies in Tajima country|Q11381626|WD=Q11381626|1=List of Shikinaisha in Tajima Province}} === External link === {{Commonscat}} *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6324077.html Mizutani Shrine] (Hyogo Prefectural Shrine Agency) == name in kana (P1814) == * みずたにじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7140005010305 == Commons category (P373) == * Mizutani-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県養父市奥米地字中島235', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Okami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Meishin University), {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}{{ill|lt=Sannomiya|Q7418864|WD=Q7418864|1=Sannomiya}}, old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Shikinaisha (Myojin Taisha)|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534}} is {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{ill|lt=Suijin|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}{{ill|lt=Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, ed. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jingi Zensho|1=UNKNOWN}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{ill|lt=Hoshokugami|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|1=Ukemochi}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|lt=Kōka Nenchu|1=Kōka}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Koan|Q389180|WD=Q389180|1=Kōan (Kamakura period)}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ota Bun of Tajima Province|1=UNKNOWN}}'' in 1994 ({{ill|lt=Kamoto|Q1327409|WD=Q1327409|1=Kagen}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shokeimonin|1=UNKNOWN}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fuzan Sakurai|1=UNKNOWN}} "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajima Ko|1=UNKNOWN}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{ill|lt=enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jakon|1=UNKNOWN}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoneji River|1=UNKNOWN}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1710|Q6861|WD=Q6861|1=1710}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{ill|lt=heiko belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=heiko belt}} on their {{ill|lt=hōji belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=hōji belt}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{ill|lt=geta|Q721941|WD=Q721941|1=Geta (footwear)}} and carrying {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinotake|1=UNKNOWN}} about 3 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|1=Metre}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{ill|lt=Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|WD=Q11666855|lt=Yabu Town|1=Yabu}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{ill|lt=katana|Q290445|WD=Q290445|1=kamishimo}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{ill|lt=katana|Q7523706|WD=Q7523706|1=Single-edged sword}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hakama|1=UNKNOWN}}'s {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matadate|1=UNKNOWN}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shoulder robe|1=UNKNOWN}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{ill|lt=sumo ring|Q832429|WD=Q832429|1=Dohyō}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{ill|lt=four forks|Q109364591|WD=Q109364591|1=shiko}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{ill|lt=headman|Q11664931|WD=Q11664931|lt=headman|1=touya}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{ill|lt=sacred sake|Q3313722|WD=Q3313722|1=Omiki}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{ill|lt=gyoji|Q907647|WD=Q907647|1=Gyōji}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{ill|lt=Magic|Q14637843|WD=Q14637843|1=Magic}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{ill|lt=sumo festival|Q11582050|WD=Q11582050|1=Sumai no Sechie}} that was held in {{ill|lt=the imperial court|Q11453221|WD=Q11453221|1=Kyūchū}} during the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{ill|lt=ukei|Q11260316|WD=Q11260316|lt=pledge (ukei)|1=Ukehi}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{ill|lt=rock-paper-scissors|Q106631|WD=Q106631|1=Rock paper scissors}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{ill|lt=parishin|Q125562175|WD=Q125562175|1=Ujiko}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{ill|lt=divine food|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{ill|lt=une|Q2262727|WD=Q2262727|1=Hilling}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{ill|lt=Main shrine|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Ichikensha Nagaruzo|1=Nagare-zukuri}} board roof, with {{ill|lt=pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399|WD=Q3105399|1=Giboshi}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hamaedoko|1=UNKNOWN}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hamadoko|1=UNKNOWN}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=covered building|1=UNKNOWN}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{ill|lt=gable-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} flat with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tiled roof|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is adjacent to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himachi|1=UNKNOWN}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1913|Q2080|WD=Q2080|1=1913}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk == name in kana (P1814) == * みずたにじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7140005010305 == Commons category (P373) == * Mizutani-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県養父市奥米地字中島235', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Okami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Meishin University), {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}{{ill|lt=Sannomiya|Q7418864|WD=Q7418864|1=Sannomiya}}, old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Shikinaisha (Myojin Taisha)|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534}} is {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{ill|lt=Suijin|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}{{ill|lt=Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, ed. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jingi Zensho|1=UNKNOWN}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{ill|lt=Hoshokugami|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|1=Ukemochi}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|lt=Kōka Nenchu|1=Kōka}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Koan|Q389180|WD=Q389180|1=Kōan (Kamakura period)}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ota Bun of Tajima Province|1=UNKNOWN}}'' in 1994 ({{ill|lt=Kamoto|Q1327409|WD=Q1327409|1=Kagen}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shokeimonin|1=UNKNOWN}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fuzan Sakurai|1=UNKNOWN}} "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajima Ko|1=UNKNOWN}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{ill|lt=enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jakon|1=UNKNOWN}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoneji River|1=UNKNOWN}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1710|Q6861|WD=Q6861|1=1710}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{ill|lt=heiko belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=heiko belt}} on their {{ill|lt=hōji belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=hōji belt}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{ill|lt=geta|Q721941|WD=Q721941|1=Geta (footwear)}} and carrying {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinotake|1=UNKNOWN}} about 3 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|1=Metre}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{ill|lt=Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|WD=Q11666855|lt=Yabu Town|1=Yabu}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{ill|lt=katana|Q290445|WD=Q290445|1=kamishimo}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{ill|lt=katana|Q7523706|WD=Q7523706|1=Single-edged sword}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hakama|1=UNKNOWN}}'s {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matadate|1=UNKNOWN}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shoulder robe|1=UNKNOWN}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{ill|lt=sumo ring|Q832429|WD=Q832429|1=Dohyō}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{ill|lt=four forks|Q109364591|WD=Q109364591|1=shiko}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{ill|lt=headman|Q11664931|WD=Q11664931|lt=headman|1=touya}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{ill|lt=sacred sake|Q3313722|WD=Q3313722|1=Omiki}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{ill|lt=gyoji|Q907647|WD=Q907647|1=Gyōji}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{ill|lt=Magic|Q14637843|WD=Q14637843|1=Magic}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{ill|lt=sumo festival|Q11582050|WD=Q11582050|1=Sumai no Sechie}} that was held in {{ill|lt=the imperial court|Q11453221|WD=Q11453221|1=Kyūchū}} during the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{ill|lt=ukei|Q11260316|WD=Q11260316|lt=pledge (ukei)|1=Ukehi}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{ill|lt=rock-paper-scissors|Q106631|WD=Q106631|1=Rock paper scissors}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{ill|lt=parishin|Q125562175|WD=Q125562175|1=Ujiko}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{ill|lt=divine food|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{ill|lt=une|Q2262727|WD=Q2262727|1=Hilling}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{ill|lt=Main shrine|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Ichikensha Nagaruzo|1=Nagare-zukuri}} board roof, with {{ill|lt=pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399|WD=Q3105399|1=Giboshi}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hamaedoko|1=UNKNOWN}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hamadoko|1=UNKNOWN}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=covered building|1=UNKNOWN}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{ill|lt=gable-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} flat with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tiled roof|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is adjacent to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himachi|1=UNKNOWN}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1913|Q2080|WD=Q2080|1=1913}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk == name in kana (P1814) == * みずたにじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 7140005010305 == Commons category (P373) == * Mizutani-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県養父市奥米地字中島235', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mizutani-jinja}} [[JA:水谷神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11549203}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Mizutani Shrine |Image = [[File:Mizutani jinjya 1.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}235 Nakajima, Okumeji |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Okami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (Meishin University), {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}{{ill|lt=Sannomiya|Q7418864|WD=Q7418864|1=Sannomiya}}, old {{ill|lt=murasha|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} Board roof |alias= |Fudasho = |Regular Festival = October Sports Day |Shinto rituals = Hoiyara dance (sasa dance), Nettei sumo (both on regular festival days) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Mizutani Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}}}} ``Mizutani Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yabu City|Q869014|WD=Q869014|1=Yabu, Hyōgo}}. It is also commonly known as ``Mitani'' Shrine. It is located in the northwestern part of Okumeji Village, facing east on the top level of the precincts divided into three levels. {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Shikinaisha (Myojin Taisha)|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534}} is {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined God === It is said that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here, but the theory is that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mizutari'' and is {{ill|lt=Suijin|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}{{ill|lt=Yuriyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, ed. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jingi Zensho|1=UNKNOWN}} (1906-1903). , the theory that ``Mizutani'' is ``Mitani'' and ``Mizutani'' is the ``Otani''. , {{ill|lt=Hoshokugami|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|1=Ukemochi}} theory``Enki'' cited in ``Yobu Gunshi'' (1928). This auspicious origin is said to have been written in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|lt=Kōka Nenchu|1=Kōka}} (mid-19th century). , etc. === History === The year of establishment is unknown. There is a theory that the god Hoshoku taught people how to cultivate rice and other crops, so the place was named ``Yonechi'' and the same god was enshrined. Furthermore, ``Meiji'' means ``a route'' and is said to be a place name based on a narrow geographical feature (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary 28 Hyogo Prefecture, Kadokawa Shoten, 1988). . It is an old shrine listed as a Meishin Taisha in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' However, it is not listed in the ``Engishiki'' Extraordinary Festival (Volume 3) Meishin Festival article. Initially, the valley to the south of the current enshrinement site, which forms the boundary between Okumechi and Chuumechi, was called ``Mizudani'', and it is said that it was enshrined above a waterfall that hangs deep in the valley.``Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I''. .Mizutani Shrine in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Koan|Q389180|WD=Q389180|1=Kōan (Kamakura period)}}8 ({{jalink|1285 ``Mizutanisha'' in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ota Bun of Tajima Province|1=UNKNOWN}}'' in 1994 ({{ill|lt=Kamoto|Q1327409|WD=Q1327409|1=Kagen}}) ({{jal Ink|1306|Q5716}})'s "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shokeimonin|1=UNKNOWN}} Ryo Catalog Proposal" appears to include "Mizutani Taisha" etc., but this is the current {{jalink |Yobu Shrine|Q11666856}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fuzan Sakurai|1=UNKNOWN}} "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajima Ko|1=UNKNOWN}}" (mid-Edo period). , it is thought to have been mixed up because at some point it was {{ill|lt=enshrined|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} at Yabu Shrine and was called ``Yaibu Mizutani Daimyojin'', and there was still a shrine dedicated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jakon|1=UNKNOWN}} in Okumeji. name="Gunshi"/>, and it is presumed that it was later enshrined there again. . After that, the shrine building was washed away by a big storm, and it was decided to be enshrined in the flat land along the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoneji River|1=UNKNOWN}}, but it was moved to its current location in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1710|Q6861|WD=Q6861|1=1710}}), according to "Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I." The above ``Engi'' states that the year of the transfer was {{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 2nd year (1717), but an inscription dated in 1717 is left in the current main shrine. .He was made a village shrine in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}). === Rituals === The annual festival was originally held on September 9th ({{ill|lt=lunar calendar|Q11511066|WD=Q11511066|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}}), then October 17th, then changed to the 23rd or 24th of the same month, and is currently held on October {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}}. It also serves as the autumn festival of the shrine Tawa Shrine in the precincts. Takashi Kushita "Festival Events and Explanation of Hyogo - Nettei Festival of Okumechi Mizutani Shrine" (included in "Festival Events, Hyogo Prefecture"). After the annual festival, ``Hoiyara Odori'' and ``Neti Sumo'', as well as children's sumo, are held. === Hoiyara dance === It is dedicated following the annual festival at the main shrine. It is also called ``Sasa Odori'', and in front of Tawa Shrine, people wear a {{ill|lt=heiko belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=heiko belt}} on their {{ill|lt=hōji belt|Q11392799|WD=Q11392799|1=hōji belt}} and carry a tenugui around their waist and {{jalink|a pack of cigarettes. It is danced by 12 men holding {{ill|lt=geta|Q721941|WD=Q721941|1=Geta (footwear)}} and carrying {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shinotake|1=UNKNOWN}} about 3 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|1=Metre}} in length. First, the dancers line up in a circle, take off their geta, and stand on top of them. Next, the players move on top of the geta while hitting the ground under Shinotake while chanting ``Yoi, yoi, yoi, yoi,'' and then circle around counterclockwise three times to return to the position where their own geta was placed.If even one person misses their step during that time, it starts over from the beginning. Next, the person puts on geta, faces the center of the circle, holds a bamboo in his left hand, and uses his right hand to check whether he has a tenugui around his waist, then transfers the bamboo to his right hand, and checks that he has a cigarette in his left hand (this is said to be an act of ``taking a puff'').If there is no abnormality, he bends the base and end of the bamboo with both hands to form a circle, and strikes the ground three times to the sound of ``Ya-ya-ya-ya!''. Finally, they finished the dance by dedicating bamboo in front of the shrine at Tawa Shrine, and then moved on to nettei sumo 's ``Adoptive Father's Festival''. . This dance was the only ritual dance in the former {{ill|lt=Yabu Town (Yabu District, Hyogo Prefecture 2004)|Q11666855|WD=Q11666855|lt=Yabu Town|1=Yabu}}. === Nettei Sumo === First, two adult men wearing {{ill|lt=katana|Q290445|WD=Q290445|1=kamishimo}} (but no lower clothes on the upper body), barefoot and holding {{ill|lt=katana|Q7523706|WD=Q7523706|1=Single-edged sword}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hakama|1=UNKNOWN}}'s {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matadate|1=UNKNOWN}}, offer the sword in front of the main shrine of Mizutani Shrine and worship, and after worshiping, receive the sword from the {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} and go down the stone steps to the middle of the precincts. On the middle stage, he takes off his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shoulder robe|1=UNKNOWN}}, wraps it around his sword, holds it in both hands, and jumps three steps with both feet toward the stone steps he came down from, places the sword on the steps, holds the sword with his right hand, and strokes his waist with his left. Next, they switch their left and right hands and perform the same gesture.Finally, they hold the sword with their right hand and stroke their hips with their left hand, then take three steps back and face each other in the center of the middle stage. There is no {{ill|lt=sumo ring|Q832429|WD=Q832429|1=Dohyō}} built in the middle plaza, but after a short bow, the match takes the same stance as {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}}, and the process proceeds as follows. #While shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi," step left and right and {{ill|lt=four forks|Q109364591|WD=Q109364591|1=shiko}}, shouting "Yoi" and step your right foot forward to the left, clenching your right hand into a fist and thrusting out. #Similarly, step on the four legs, say "yoi", and do the left and right movements in the opposite direction. #Similarly, step forward, hold the opponent's neck with your left hand, place your right hand on your waist, and while shouting "Yoi, yoi, yoi", jump around to the left and return to the original position. #Similarly, step on the four legs, switch left and right hands, and turn once to the right. #Finally, step on the same four steps, hold the opponent's shoulders with both hands, turn once to the left in the same way, return to the original position, place both hands on your hips, and bow to the left and right to finish.After finishing the sumo match, the two men placed the swords on the stone steps, wrapped them in shoulder gi, and held them in both hands. They ascended to the front of the main shrine and deposited them in the worship hall. Children's sumo was then held in a ring set up at the bottom of the temple grounds. In the past, the sumo wrestler was chosen from the next ``miya duty'', {{ill|lt=headman|Q11664931|WD=Q11664931|lt=headman|1=touya}}, also called ``oto''. After the sumo match, a drawing of lots was held at the shrine to determine the head of the following year, and an ``otowatashi'' event was held where they drank {{ill|lt=sacred sake|Q3313722|WD=Q3313722|1=Omiki}}.Agency for Cultural Affairs/Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, "[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/159968 Foster Father's Nettei Sumo]" (Cultural Heritage Online) (accessed April 20, 2010). .Nettei sumo is a special type of {{ill|lt=sumo|Q40561|WD=Q40561|1=Sumo}} that does not decide the match, and there is no ring or {{ill|lt=gyoji|Q907647|WD=Q907647|1=Gyōji}}, and it is a ritual to appease the evil spirits of the land and give thanks for the fertility of the crops. or {{ill|lt=Magic|Q14637843|WD=Q14637843|1=Magic}} Sumo Shinto ritual. , is said to be the prototype of the current sumo, descending from the {{ill|lt=sumo festival|Q11582050|WD=Q11582050|1=Sumai no Sechie}} that was held in {{ill|lt=the imperial court|Q11453221|WD=Q11453221|1=Kyūchū}} during the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. , the national "{{jal Ink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property for which measures such as record-keeping should be taken|Q11642584}}", and on March 9, 2016, it was designated as Hyogo Prefecture's {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=UNKNOWN}}.Currently, it is not a form of deciding the outcome, but in the past, it seems that the winner was the one who correctly returned to the original position after crossing their shoulders at the end and turning from side to side. It is also said that it has its origins in the {{ill|lt=ukei|Q11260316|WD=Q11260316|lt=pledge (ukei)|1=Ukehi}}, in which when it was necessary to make a choice, a representative was selected and made to compete, and the will of the gods was determined based on the result. According to this, the fist in this sumo wrestling was {{ill|lt=rock-paper-scissors|Q106631|WD=Q106631|1=Rock paper scissors}}, and when the match was not won in ``Aiko,'' it was a match in which the wrestlers linked their shoulders and went around.This is why there is no ring, and the reason there is no referee is because the audience could easily make a decision. In addition, ``netei'' is said to be a corruption of ``nite sumo'' and ``netae sumo,'' and it is also said to originate from the repeated four-legged movement repeated in this region, which is called ``nerite''. === Miyaza === Currently, the management of the shrine is {{ill|lt=parishin|Q125562175|WD=Q125562175|1=Ujiko}}, and each household is divided into 10 groups, and each group takes turns every year to clean the shrine, prepare {{ill|lt=divine food|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}} for the festival, take care of the nettei sumo wrestlers, etc. (previously one group was responsible for this, but now two groups are responsible), and this is called ``Miya duty.'' In the past, a group called ``Mizutani-ko'' was organized, and a shrine duty was selected from among the lecturers, and the shrine duty cultivated the Miyaden 7 {{ill|lt=une|Q2262727|WD=Q2262727|1=Hilling}} owned by the shrine and used it to cover part of the festival's expenses . === Shrine building === {{ill|lt=Main shrine|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is {{ill|lt=Nagaruzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Ichikensha Nagaruzo|1=Nagare-zukuri}} board roof, with {{ill|lt=pseudo Hojyu|Q3105399|WD=Q3105399|1=Giboshi}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hamaedoko|1=UNKNOWN}} with a rim (large platform) is turned around, and a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hamadoko|1=UNKNOWN}} (hamadoko) is placed below the ascending stage set in front. Although it is small in size, it is in good condition because it is inside a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=covered building|1=UNKNOWN}}. The inscription in ink indicates that the building was built in the 7th year of the Hoei era, and the detailed carvings have a unique style.Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia, Volume 2, Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1981. . The roofed roof is {{ill|lt=gable-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} flat with a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=tiled roof|1=UNKNOWN}}, and it is adjacent to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} (built on June 17, 1909) of the tsumairi, which also has a gabled tile roof. The community center built at the bottom of the precincts is a renovated dance hall where wakes for the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Himachi|1=UNKNOWN}} were once held. . === Precinct Company === Sanbashira Shrine and Tawa Shrine are enshrined to the left (south) of the main hall, and Yamanoguchi Shrine and Yabashira Shrine to the right. Tawa Shrine was moved from Okumeji Aza Tawa to the precincts in {{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1913|Q2080|WD=Q2080|1=1913}}), Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Kodo, and Mitsubashira Shrine was moved from Aza Inokuchi. === Cultural assets === *Nettei Sumo (National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property, Hyogo Prefecture Designated Intangible Folk
